```{r}
  full_join(lower_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(lowerN_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(upper_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(upperN_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(se_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(seN_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(t_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(tN_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(stdEst_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(stdEstLower_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(stdEstUpper_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(r_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>%  
  full_join(r2_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(r2Adj_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(r2AdjN_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(aic_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(rmse_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(f_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(df1_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(df2_long,by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
 
  
 
 full_join(p_value_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
 full_join(p_valueN_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 

 full_join(p.value_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
 full_join(p.valueN_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) 





# Assign final datasets to the global environment
assign_df("origCoef", origCoef)
assign_df("repCoef", repCoef)
assign_df("fit_orig", origfit)
assign_df("fit_rep", repfit)
assign_df("anova_orig", origANOVA)
assign_df("anova_rep", repANOVA)
assign_df("fdat", fdat)
assign_df("ffdat", ffdat)



```

```{r}
# Function to dynamically assign data frames to the global environment
assign_df <- function(name, df) {
  assign(name, df, envir = .GlobalEnv)
}

# Function to dynamically fetch a data frame
get_dynamic_df <- function(df_name) {
  get(df_name, envir = .GlobalEnv)
}

# Get the unique model numbers
model_numbers <- unique(fdat$Model)

# Split the fdat data frame by Model and assign each subset to the global environment
split_fdat <- split(fdat, fdat$Model)

# Dynamically assign each split dataset to the global environment
for (i in seq_along(model_numbers)) {
  model_number <- model_numbers[i]
  assign_df(paste0("fdat", model_number), split_fdat[[i]])
}



# Function to generate a forest plot
generate_forest_plot <- function(model_num) {
  original_data <- get_dynamic_df(paste0("O_coefficients_raw_df_", model_num)) %>%
    dplyr::select(n, term, est, lower, upper) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Source = "Original") %>%
    dplyr::mutate(est = as.numeric(est), lower = as.numeric(lower), upper = as.numeric(upper)) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Order = 0)  # Set Order to 0 for Original
  
  reproduced_data <- get_dynamic_df(paste0("R_coefficients_raw_df_", model_num)) %>%
    dplyr::select(n, term, est, lower, upper) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Source = "Reproduced") %>%
    dplyr::mutate(est = as.numeric(est), lower = as.numeric(lower), upper = as.numeric(upper)) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Order = 1)  # Set Order to 1 for Reproduced
  
  combined_data <- dplyr::bind_rows(original_data, reproduced_data) %>%
    dplyr::filter(term != "Intercept") %>%
    dplyr::mutate(OrderHelper = n + Order / 1000) %>%
    dplyr::arrange(OrderHelper) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Label = paste(term, Source, sep = " - ")) %>%
    dplyr::arrange(desc(OrderHelper))  # Reverse the order of the data
  
  # Set the factor levels based on reversed order
  combined_data$Label <- factor(combined_data$Label, levels = combined_data$Label)
  
  
  plot <- ggplot2::ggplot(combined_data, ggplot2::aes(x = est, y = Label)) +
    ggplot2::geom_errorbar(ggplot2::aes(xmin = lower, xmax = upper, color = Source), width = 0.2) +
    ggplot2::geom_point(ggplot2::aes(color = Source), size = 3) +
    ggplot2::scale_color_manual(values = c("Original" = "darkblue", "Reproduced" = "darkred")) +
    ggplot2::labs(x = "Coefficient", y = "") +
    ggplot2::theme_minimal() +
    ggplot2::theme(
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      panel.background = element_blank(),
      plot.background = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
      legend.position = "top",
      axis.line = ggplot2::element_line(color = "black")
    )
  
  ggplot2::ggsave(paste0("figures/forest_plot_model_", model_num, ".png"), plot = plot)
}


# Function to generate Bland-Altman plot using blandr with error handling
generate_bland_altman_plot <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  tryCatch({
    # Perform Bland-Altman analysis to get statistics
    blandr_results <- blandr::blandr.statistics(
      method1 = fdat$R_p.valueN,
      method2 = fdat$O_p.valueN
    )
    
    # Generate the Bland-Altman plot without default annotations
    plot <- blandr::blandr.draw(
      fdat$R_p.valueN, 
      fdat$O_p.valueN, 
      ciDisplay = FALSE, 
      ciShading = FALSE,
      plotTitle = paste("")
    )
    
    # Add custom annotations with text above the lines, rounded to three decimals
    plot <- plot +
      ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = blandr_results$bias, color = "blue", linetype = "dashed") +
      ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = blandr_results$upperLOA, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
      ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = blandr_results$lowerLOA, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
      ggplot2::annotate("text", x = max(fdat$R_p.valueN, na.rm = TRUE), y = blandr_results$bias + 0.01, 
                        label = paste("Mean Bias:", Sround(blandr_results$bias, 3)), vjust = -0.5, hjust = 1) +
      ggplot2::annotate("text", x = max(fdat$R_p.valueN, na.rm = TRUE), y = blandr_results$upperLOA + 0.01, 
                        label = paste("Upper LoA:", Sround(blandr_results$upperLOA, 3)), vjust = -0.5, hjust = 1) +
      ggplot2::annotate("text", x = max(fdat$R_p.valueN, na.rm = TRUE), y = blandr_results$lowerLOA + 0.01, 
                        label = paste("Lower LoA:", Sround(blandr_results$lowerLOA, 3)), vjust = -0.5, hjust = 1) +
      ggplot2::labs(
        x = "Mean of Reproduced and Original",
        y = "Difference (Reproduced - Original)"
      ) +
      ggplot2::theme_minimal() +
      ggplot2::theme(
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_blank(),
        plot.background = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
        panel.border = ggplot2::element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, size = 1),
        axis.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 12),
        axis.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 14),
        plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 16)
      )
    
    # Save the plot
    ggplot2::ggsave(filename = paste0("figures/bland_altman_plot_model_", model_num, ".png"), plot = plot, width = 7.74, height = 5.47)
  }, error = function(e) {
    message(paste("Bland-Altman plot failed for model", model_num, ": ", e$message))
  })
}


# Function to create and assign the CC table
create_cc_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  CC <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::select(term, O_estN, R_estN, changeB, result_O_est_category) %>%
    mutate(across(c(R_estN, changeB), ~ Sround(.x, thresround))) %>% 
    mutate(changeB = ifelse(changeB=="NA", "", changeB)) %>% 
     mutate(O_estN = ifelse(O_estN=="NA", "", O_estN)) %>%
     mutate(R_estN = ifelse(R_estN=="NA", "", R_estN)) %>%
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "result_O_est_category", width = 2.5) %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(O_estN = "O_B", R_estN = "R_B", changeB = "Change.B", result_O_est_category = "reproduce.B") %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines("O_B = original B; R_B = reproduced B; Change.B = change in R_B - O_B; Reproduce.B = B reproduced.")
  
  assign_df(paste0("CC_model_", model_num), CC)
}

# Function to create and assign the CL table
create_cl_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  CL <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::select(term, O_lowerN, R_lowerN, change.lci, result_O_lower_category) %>%
    mutate(across(c(R_lowerN, change.lci), ~ Sround(.x, thresround))) %>% 
    mutate(change.lci = ifelse(change.lci=="NA", "", change.lci)) %>% 
    mutate(R_lowerN = ifelse(R_lowerN=="NA", "", R_lowerN)) %>%
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "result_O_lower_category", width = 2.5) %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(O_lowerN= "O_lower", R_lowerN= "R_lower",  change.lci = "Change.lci", result_O_lower_category = "Reproduce.lower") %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines("O_lower = original lower confidence interval; R_lower = reproduced lower confidence interval; change.lci = change in R_lower - O_lower; Reproduce.lower = lower confidence interval reproduced.")
  
  assign_df(paste0("CL_model_", model_num), CL)
}

# Function to create and assign the CU table
create_cu_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  CU <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::select(term, O_upperN, R_upperN, change.uci, result_O_upper_category) %>%
     mutate(across(c(R_upperN, change.uci), ~ Sround(.x, thresround))) %>% 
     mutate(change.uci = ifelse(change.uci=="NA", "", change.uci)) %>% 
    mutate(R_upperN = ifelse(R_upperN=="NA", "", R_upperN)) %>%
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "result_O_upper_category", width = 2.5) %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(O_upperN= "O_upper", R_upperN= "R_upper", change.uci = "Change.uci", result_O_upper_category = "Reproduce.upper") %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines("O_upper = original upper confidence interval; R_upper = reproduced upper confidence interval; change.uci = change in R_upper - O_upper; Reproduce.upper = upper confidence interval reproduced.")
  
  assign_df(paste0("CU_model_", model_num), CU)
}


# Function to create and assign the CL table
create_se_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  SE <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::select(term, O_seN, R_seN, change.se, result_O_se_category) %>%
    mutate(across(c(R_seN, change.se), ~ Sround(.x, thresround))) %>% 
    mutate(change.se = ifelse(change.se=="NA", "", change.se)) %>% 
    mutate(R_seN = ifelse(R_seN=="NA", "", R_seN)) %>% 
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "result_O_se_category", width = 2.5) %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(O_seN= "O_SE", R_seN= "R_SE",  change.se = "Change.SE", result_O_se_category = "Reproduce.SE") %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines("O_SE = original standard error; R_SE = reproduced standard error; Change.SE = change in R_SE - O_SE; Reproduce.lower = standard error reproduced.")
  
  assign_df(paste0("SE_model_", model_num), SE)
}

# Function to create and assign the CL table
create_t_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  t <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::select(term, O_tN, R_tN, change.t, result_O_t_category) %>%
    mutate(across(c(R_tN, change.t), ~ Sround(.x, thresround))) %>% 
    mutate(change.t = ifelse(change.t =="NA", "", change.t)) %>% 
    mutate(R_tN = ifelse(R_tN=="NA", "", R_tN)) %>% 
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "result_O_t_category", width = 2.5) %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(O_tN= "O_t", R_tN= "R_t",  change.t = "Change.t", result_O_t_category = "Reproduce.t") %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines("O_t = original t value; R_t = reproduced t value; Change.t = change in R_t - O_t; Reproduce.lower = t value reproduced.")
  
  assign_df(paste0("t_model_", model_num), t)
}


# Function to create and assign the FF table
create_r2_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  R2 <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::filter(n == 1) %>%
    dplyr::select(O_r2N, R_r2N, changeR2, result_O_r2_category) %>%
    mutate(across(c(R_r2N, changeR2), ~ Sround(.x, thresround))) %>% 
    mutate(O_r2N = ifelse(O_r2N =="NA", "", O_r2N)) %>% 
      mutate(changeR2 = ifelse(changeR2=="NA", "", changeR2)) %>% 
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "O_r2N", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("O_R", as_sup("2"))) %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "R_r2N", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("R_R", as_sup("2"))) %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "changeR2", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("Change.R", as_sup("2"))) %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "result_O_r2_category", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("Reproduce.R", as_sup("2"))) %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "result_O_r2_category", width = 2.5) %>%
    flextable::  colformat_double(digits = 3) %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines(
  as_paragraph(
    "O_R", as_sup("2"), " = original R", as_sup("2"), 
    "; R_R", as_sup("2"), " = reproduced R", as_sup("2"), 
    "; Change.R", as_sup("2"), " = change in R_R", as_sup("2"), " - O_R", as_sup("2")
  )
)
  
  assign_df(paste0("R2_model_", model_num),  R2)
}


# Function to create and assign the FF table
create_r2Adj_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  R2Adj <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::filter(n == 1) %>%
    dplyr::select(O_r2AdjN, R_r2AdjN, changeR2Adj, result_O_r2Adj_category) %>%
    mutate(across(c(R_r2AdjN, changeR2Adj), ~ Sround(.x, thresround))) %>% 
    mutate(O_r2AdjN = ifelse(O_r2AdjN =="NA", "", O_r2AdjN)) %>% 
      mutate(changeR2Adj = ifelse(changeR2Adj=="NA", "", changeR2Adj)) %>% 
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "O_r2AdjN", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("O_R", as_sup("2"), "Adj")) %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "R_r2AdjN", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("R_R", as_sup("2"), "Adj")) %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "changeR2Adj", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("Change.R", as_sup("2"), "Adj")) %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "result_O_r2Adj_category", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("Reproduce.R", as_sup("2"), "Adj")) %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "result_O_r2Adj_category", width = 2.5) %>%
    flextable::  colformat_double(digits = 3) %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines(
        as_paragraph(
            "O_R", as_sup("2"), "Adj = original R", as_sup("2"), " Adjusted", 
            "; R_R", as_sup("2"), "Adj = reproduced R", as_sup("2"), " Adjusted", 
            "; Change.R", as_sup("2"), "Adj = change in R", as_sup("2"), "Adj (R", as_sup("2"), "Adj  - O_R", as_sup("2"), "Adj)",
            "; Reproduce.R", as_sup("2"), "Adj = R", as_sup("2"), " Adjusted reproduced."
        )
    )
  assign_df(paste0("R2Adj_model_", model_num),  R2Adj)
}

# Function to create and assign the DF table
create_df_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  DF <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::filter(n == 1) %>%
    dplyr::select(O_df1, R_df1, Change.df1, Reproduce.df1, O_df2, R_df2, Change.df2, Reproduce.df2) %>%
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(O_df1 = "O_DF1", R_df1 = "R_DF1", Change.df1 = "Change.DF1", Reproduce.df1 = "Reproduce.DF1", O_df2 = "O_DF2", R_df2 = "R_DF2", Change.df2 = "Change.DF2", Reproduce.df2 = "Reproduce.DF2") %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines("O_DF1 = original degrees of freedom for the model; R_DF1 = reproduced degrees of freedom for the model; Change.DF1 = change in R_DF1 - O_DF1; Reproduce.DF1 = reproduced degrees of freedom for the model (R_DF1 = O_DF1); O_DF2 = original degrees of freedom for the residuals; R_DF2 = reproduced degrees of freedom for the residuals; Change.DF2 = change in R_DF2 - O_DF2; Reproduce.DF2 = reproduced degrees of freedom for the residuals (R_DF2 = O_DF2).")
  
  assign_df(paste0("DF_model_", model_num), DF)
}

create_cp_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  Cp <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::select(term, O_p.value, R_p.valueN, changep, result_O_p.value_category, SigChangeDirection) %>%
    mutate(changep = Sround(changep, 4)) %>%
    mutate(R_p.valueN = sapply(R_p.valueN, round_p)) %>%
    mutate(O_p.value = ifelse(O_p.value == "NA", "", O_p.value)) %>%
    mutate(changep = ifelse(changep == "NA", "", changep)) %>%
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "SigChangeDirection", width = 3) %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(term = "Term",  O_p.value = "O_p", R_p.valueN="R_p", changep = "Change.p", result_O_p.value_category = "Reproduce.p") %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines("O_p = original p-value; R_p = reproduced p-value; Changep = change in p-value R_p - O_p; Reproduce.p = p-values reproduced. SigChangeDirection = statistical significance and B change between original and reproduced models. Note, p-values that were <0.001 were set to 0.00099 for the purposes of comparison.") %>%
    flextable::  colformat_double(digits = 3)
  
  assign_df(paste0("Cp_model_", model_num), Cp)
}


 #Function to create and assign the FF table
create_ff_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  FF <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::filter(n == 1) %>%
    dplyr::select(term, O_f, R_fN, Change.f, result_O_f_category) %>%
    mutate(across(c(R_fN, Change.f), ~ Sround(.x, thresround))) %>% 
    mutate(Change.f = ifelse(Change.f=="NA", "", Change.f)) %>% 
    mutate(R_fN = ifelse(R_fN =="NA", "", R_fN)) %>% 
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "result_O_f_category", width = 2.5) %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(
      term = "Term",
      O_f = "O_F",
      R_fN = "R_F",
      Change.f = "Change.F",
      result_O_f_category = "Reproduce.F"
    ) %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines("O_F = original global F; R_F = reproduced global F; Change.F = change in R_F - O_F; Reproduce.F = Global F reproduced.")
  
  assign_df(paste0("FF_model_", model_num), FF)
}




# Generate model outputs
generate_model_outputs <- function(model_num) {
  generate_forest_plot(model_num)
  fdat <- get_dynamic_df(paste0("fdat", model_num))
  generate_bland_altman_plot(fdat, model_num)
  create_cc_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_cl_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_cu_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_se_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_t_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_r2_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_r2Adj_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_ff_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_df_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_cp_table(fdat, model_num)
}

# Example list of model numbers (you can define this based on your actual models)
model_numbers <- 1:num_models

# Call the function for each model
for (i in model_numbers) {
  generate_model_outputs(i)
}


```

```{r M1Rassump}

R_description_raw1 <- R_description_flextable_1$body$dataset


# Create an empty dataset for assumptions
M1Rassump<- data.frame(matrix(nrow = nrow(O_anova_raw_df_1), ncol = 25))
# Assign column names
names(M1Rassump) <- c("paper_ID",  "Model", "origin", "n", "Term", "linearplot", "linear",  "homtest", "homplot", "hom",  "VIF", "KS", "SW", "QQ", "normal", 
                      "collin", "indDes", "indtest", "indep", "R2", "cooks", "DFFIT", "DFbeta", "CovRatio", "Outlier") 


M1Rassump<-M1Rassump %>% mutate(Model=1, origin="Reproduced", n=1:nrow(.))
M1Rassump$paper_ID <- paper_ID
M1lintest<-R_residuals_raw_df_1 %>% dplyr::select(n, Model, paper_ID,origin, Results) %>% rename("lineartest"="Results") %>% 
  mutate(lineartest= case_when(lineartest== "Linearity violation" ~ "Violation", lineartest==  "No linearity violation"~ "No Violation", TRUE ~ NA_character_))
M1Rassump <-merge(M1Rassump, M1lintest, by=c("paper_ID", "Model", "origin", "n"))
M1colin<- R_collinearity_raw_df_1 %>%  add_row(.before = 1) %>% mutate(Model = 1, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Reproduced", n=1:nrow(O_anova_raw_df_1)) %>% 
  dplyr::select(vif, n, Model, paper_ID,origin) 
M1colin$paper_ID <- paper_ID
M1Rassump <-merge(M1Rassump, M1colin, by=c("paper_ID", "Model", "origin", "n"))
M1Rassump$Term <- O_anova_raw_df_1$term[order(O_anova_raw_df_1$n)]

# note some variables on a overall model level some at variable level, if this is the case n=1 is the overall model for example linearity. DFBeta is measured at coeffcient level but has been made to model level.
M1Rassump<-M1Rassump %>%
mutate(linear= case_when(n==1 | n==2  ~ "Violation")) %>%   # not automatic based on overall information  
mutate(homtest = ifelse(R_homoscedasticity_raw_df_1$p.value < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(homplot = "Violation", hom="Violation") %>% # not automatic
mutate(linearplot= case_when(n==1 | n==2  ~ "Violation")) %>% # not automatic
mutate(KS = ifelse(R_normality_ks_raw_df_1$p.value < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(SW = ifelse(R_normality_sw_raw_df_1$p.value < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(QQ = "Violation", normal="Violation") %>% # not automatic
mutate(indtest = ifelse(R_independence_raw_df_1$p < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(VIF = ifelse(vif < 5, "No Violation", "Violation")) %>% 
mutate(collin = "NA") %>%  # not automatic based on overall information, including assessing SE if problem
mutate(indDes = "Violation", indep="Violation") %>% # not automatic
dplyr::select(-vif) %>%  
mutate(R2 = "NA") 


DFbeta_table1 <- R_description_raw1 %>%
  filter(str_starts(term, "dfb.")) %>%
  mutate(
    Term = str_remove(term, "^dfb\\."),
    Term = if_else(Term == "1_", "Intercept", Term),
    DFbeta = if_else(min <= -1 | max >= 1, "Yes", "No")
  )%>% dplyr::select(Term, DFbeta)
DFbeta_table1$Term<-R_coefficients_raw_df_1$term[!is.na(R_coefficients_raw_df_1$est)]



M1Rassump$DFbeta <- if_else(
  any(DFbeta_table1$DFbeta == "Yes", na.rm = TRUE),
  "Yes",
  "No"
)

M1Rassump <- M1Rassump |>
  mutate(
    cooks = if_else(flag_any(R_description_raw1, ".cooksd", max >= 0.5),
                    "Yes", "No")) |>
  mutate(DFFIT = if_else(
      flag_any(R_description_raw1, "dffit", min <= -1 | max >= 1),
      "Yes", "No"),
    CovRatio = if_else(
      flag_any(R_description_raw1, "cov.r", min <= 0.9 | max >= 1.1),
      "Yes", "No"))|>
  dplyr::mutate(
    Outlier = if_else(
      any(cooks == "Yes" | DFbeta == "Yes" | DFFIT == "Yes"),
      "Yes", "No")) |> 
  arrange(n)



```

```{r M2Rassump}
R_description_raw2 <- R_description_flextable_2$body$dataset



# Create an empty dataset for assumptions
M2Rassump<- data.frame(matrix(nrow = nrow(O_anova_raw_df_2), ncol = 25))
# Assign column names
names(M2Rassump) <- c("paper_ID",  "Model", "origin", "n", "Term", "linearplot", "linear",  "homtest", "homplot", "hom",  "VIF", "KS", "SW", "QQ", "normal", 
                      "collin", "indDes", "indtest", "indep", "R2", "cooks", "DFFIT", "DFbeta", "CovRatio", "Outlier")  


M2Rassump<-M2Rassump %>% mutate(Model=2, origin="Reproduced",  n=1:nrow(.))
M2Rassump$paper_ID <- paper_ID
M2lintest<-R_residuals_raw_df_2 %>% dplyr::select(n, Model, paper_ID,origin, Results) %>% rename("lineartest"="Results") %>% 
  mutate(lineartest= case_when(lineartest== "Linearity violation" ~ "Violation", lineartest==  "No linearity violation"~ "No Violation", TRUE ~ NA_character_))
M2Rassump <-merge(M2Rassump, M2lintest, by=c("paper_ID", "Model", "origin", "n"))
M2colin<- R_collinearity_raw_df_2 %>%  add_row(.before = 1) %>% mutate(Model = 2, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Reproduced", n=1:nrow(O_anova_raw_df_2)) %>% 
  dplyr::select(vif, n, Model, paper_ID,origin) 
M2colin$paper_ID <- paper_ID
M2Rassump <-merge(M2Rassump, M2colin, by=c("paper_ID", "Model", "origin", "n"))
M2Rassump$Term <- O_anova_raw_df_2$term[order(O_anova_raw_df_2$n)]

# note some variables on a overall model level some at variable level, if this is the case n=1 is the overall model for example linearity. DFBeta is measured at coeffcient level but has been made to model level.
M2Rassump<-M2Rassump %>%
mutate(linearplot= case_when(n==1 | n==2   ~ "Violation")) %>% # not automatic
mutate(linear= case_when(n==1 | n==2   ~ "Violation")) %>%   # not automatic based on overall information  
mutate(homtest = ifelse(R_homoscedasticity_raw_df_2$p.value < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(homplot = "No Violation", hom="No Violation") %>% # not automatic
mutate(KS = ifelse(R_normality_ks_raw_df_2$p.value < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(SW = ifelse(R_normality_sw_raw_df_2$p.value < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(QQ = "Violation", normal="Violation") %>% # not automatic
mutate(indtest = ifelse(R_independence_raw_df_2$p < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(VIF = ifelse(vif < 5, "No Violation", "Violation")) %>% 
mutate(collin = "NA") %>%  # not automatic based on overall information, including assessing SE if problem
mutate(indDes = "Violation", indep="Violation") %>% # not automatic
dplyr::select(-vif) %>%  
mutate(R2 = "NA") 


DFbeta_table2 <- R_description_raw2 %>%
  filter(str_starts(term, "dfb.")) %>%
  mutate(
    Term = str_remove(term, "^dfb\\."),
    Term = if_else(Term == "1_", "Intercept", Term),
    DFbeta = if_else(min <= -1 | max >= 1, "Yes", "No")
  )%>% dplyr::select(Term, DFbeta)
DFbeta_table2$Term<-R_coefficients_raw_df_2$term[!is.na(R_coefficients_raw_df_2$est)]



M2Rassump$DFbeta <- if_else(
  any(DFbeta_table2$DFbeta == "Yes", na.rm = TRUE),
  "Yes",
  "No"
)

M2Rassump <- M2Rassump |>
  mutate(
    cooks = if_else(flag_any(R_description_raw2, ".cooksd", max >= 0.5),
                    "Yes", "No")) |>
  mutate(DFFIT = if_else(
      flag_any(R_description_raw2, "dffit", min <= -1 | max >= 1),
      "Yes", "No"),
    CovRatio = if_else(
      flag_any(R_description_raw2, "cov.r", min <= 0.9 | max >= 1.1),
      "Yes", "No"))|>
  dplyr::mutate(
    Outlier = if_else(
      any(cooks == "Yes" | DFbeta == "Yes" | DFFIT == "Yes"),
      "Yes", "No")) |> arrange(n)

 

```

```{r M3Rassump}

R_description_raw3 <- R_description_flextable_3$body$dataset
# Create an empty dataset for assumptions
M3Rassump<- data.frame(matrix(nrow = nrow(O_anova_raw_df_3), ncol = 24))
# Assign column names
names(M3Rassump) <- c("paper_ID",  "Model", "origin", "n", "Term", "linearplot", "linear",  "homtest", "homplot", "hom",  "VIF", "KS", "SW", "QQ", "normal", 
                      "collin", "indDes", "indtest", "indep", "R2", "cooks", "DFFIT", "CovRatio", "Outlier") 


M3Rassump<-M3Rassump %>% mutate(Model=3, origin="Reproduced", n=1:nrow(O_anova_raw_df_3))
M3Rassump$paper_ID <- paper_ID
M3lintest<-R_residuals_raw_df_3 %>% dplyr::select(n, Model, paper_ID,origin, Results) %>% rename("lineartest"="Results") %>% 
  mutate(lineartest= case_when(lineartest== "Linearity violation" ~ "Violation", lineartest==  "No linearity violation"~ "No Violation", TRUE ~ NA_character_))
M3Rassump <-merge(M3Rassump, M3lintest, by=c("paper_ID", "Model", "origin", "n"))
M3colin<- R_collinearity_raw_df_3 %>%  add_row(.before = 1) %>% mutate(Model = 3, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Reproduced", n=1:nrow(O_anova_raw_df_3)) %>% 
  dplyr::select(vif, n, Model, paper_ID,origin) 
M3colin$paper_ID <- paper_ID
M3Rassump <-merge(M3Rassump, M3colin, by=c("paper_ID", "Model", "origin", "n"))
M3Rassump$Term<-R_coefficients_raw_df_3$term

# note some variables on a overall model level some at variable level, if this is the case n=1 is the overall model for example linearity
M3Rassump<-M3Rassump %>%
mutate(linearplot= case_when(n==1 | n==2   ~ "Violation")) %>% # not automatic
mutate(linear= case_when(n==1 | n==2 ~ "Violation")) %>%   # not automatic based on overall information  
mutate(homtest = ifelse(R_homoscedasticity_raw_df_3$p.value < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(homplot = "No Violation", hom="No Violation") %>% # not automatic
mutate(KS = ifelse(R_normality_ks_raw_df_3$p.value < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(SW = ifelse(R_normality_sw_raw_df_3$p.value < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(QQ = "Violation", normal="Violation") %>% # not automatic
mutate(indtest = ifelse(R_independence_raw_df_3$p < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(VIF = ifelse(vif < 5, "No Violation", "Violation")) %>% 
mutate(collin = "NA") %>%  # not automatic based on overall information, including assessing SE if problem
mutate(indDes = "Violation", indep="Violation") %>% # not automatic
dplyr::select(-vif) %>%  
mutate(R2 = "NA") 


DFbeta_table3 <- R_description_raw3 %>%
  filter(str_starts(term, "dfb.")) %>%
  mutate(
    Term = str_remove(term, "^dfb\\."),
    Term = if_else(Term == "1_", "Intercept", Term),
    DFbeta = if_else(min <= -1 | max >= 1, "Yes", "No")
  )%>% dplyr::select(Term, DFbeta)
DFbeta_table3$Term<-R_coefficients_raw_df_3$term[!is.na(R_coefficients_raw_df_3$est)]



M3Rassump$DFbeta <- if_else(
  any(DFbeta_table3$DFbeta == "Yes", na.rm = TRUE),
  "Yes",
  "No"
)

M3Rassump <- M3Rassump |>
  mutate(
    cooks = if_else(flag_any(R_description_raw3, ".cooksd", max >= 0.5),
                    "Yes", "No")) |>
  mutate(DFFIT = if_else(
      flag_any(R_description_raw3, "dffit", min <= -1 | max >= 1),
      "Yes", "No"),
    CovRatio = if_else(
      flag_any(R_description_raw3, "cov.r", min <= 0.9 | max >= 1.1),
      "Yes", "No")) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    Outlier = if_else(
      any(cooks == "Yes" | DFbeta == "Yes" | DFFIT == "Yes"),
      "Yes", "No")) |> arrange(n)



```

# Model 1

::: panel-tabset
### Original results

##### Model results for `r namey1`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
O_coefficients_flextable_1
```

##### Fit statistics for `r namey1`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
O_fit_stats_flextable_1
```

##### ANOVA table for `r namey1`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
O_anova_flextable_1
```

### Reproduced results

##### Model results for `r namey1`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_coefficients_flextable_1
```

##### Fit statistics for `r namey1`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_fit_stats_flextable_1
```

##### ANOVA table for `r namey1`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_anova_flextable_1

```

##### Visualisation of regression model

The blue line shows the best line of fit with shading representing 95% confidence intervals, while holding all other covariates constant. The dots show partial residuals, which reflect the observed data adjusted for all other predictors except the one being plotted.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/reg_plot_1.png') 
```

##### Checking residuals plots for patterns

Blue line showing quadratic fit for residuals

```{r , out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/res_plot_1.png') 
```

##### Testing residuals for non linear relationships

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_residuals_flextable_1
```

##### Checking univariate relationships with the dependent variable using scatterplots

Blue line shows linear relationship, red line indicates relationship inferred by GAM modelling

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/scatter_plot_1.png') 
```

##### Linearity results

-   `r linno`
-   The outlier in this data is causing linearity problems with the regression line being pulled down so it does not fit the data.

##### Testing for homoscedasticity

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_homoscedasticity_flextable_1
```

##### Homoscedasticity results

-   `r ifelse(R_homoscedasticity_raw_df_1$p.value[1] < 0.05, "The studentized Breusch-Pagan test indicates heteroscedasticity.", "The studentized Breusch-Pagan test supports homoscedasticity.")`
-   `r somehet`

##### Model descriptives including cook's distance and leverage to understand outliers

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_description_flextable_1

```

##### Cooks threshold

Cook's distance measures the overall change in fit, if the *ith* observation is removed. Potential influential observations are identified by $\text{Cook's Distance}_i > \frac{4}{n}$, where *n* is the number of observations. In practice a threshold of 0.5 to 1 is often used to identify influential observations.

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}

knitr::include_graphics('figures/cooks_threshold_1.png')
```

##### DFFIT threshold

DFFIT measures how many standard deviations the fitted values will change when the *ith* observation is removed. Potential influential observations $\left| \text{DFFITS}_i \right| > \frac{2\sqrt{p}}{\sqrt{n}}$ where *p* is the number of predictors (including the intercept) and *n* is the number of observations. In practice this can result in a large number of points identified, a practical cut-off of 1 was used to flag observations with meaningful impact.

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/dffit_threshold_1.png')

```

##### DFBETA threshold

DFBETAS quantify the influence of the *ith* observation on the *jth* regression coefficient as the change in that coefficient when the observation is omitted, expressed in units of the coefficient’s estimated standard error. There is a DFBETA for each parameter in the model. Potential influential observations $|\text{DFBETA}_{ij}| > \frac{2}{\sqrt{n}}$, where *n* is the number of observations. In larger datasets, this threshold can flag a high number of observations with only minor influence on the model. A practical cut-off of 1 was used to flag observations with meaningful impact.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/dbetas_threshold_1.png') 

```

##### Influence plot

Observations with high leverage (horizontal) and large residuals (vertical, typically at ±2 or ±3 studentized residuals) are concerning, as they may disproportionately influence the model. This combination is reflected by large bubbles with high Cook's distance indicated by darker shadings of blue.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/cooks_plots_1.png') 

```

##### COVRATIO plot

COVRATIO measures the overall change in the precision (covariance matrix) of the estimated regression coefficients when the ith observation is removed. Values close to 1 indicate little influence on the model’s precision. Values below 1 suggest that an observation inflates the variances and reduces precision, resulting in wider confidence intervals, whereas values above 1 suggest deflated variances and narrower confidence intervals. A commonly cited guideline is $\left|\mathrm{COVRATIO}_i - 1\right| > \frac{3p}{n}$, where *p* is the number of parameters and *n* is the number of observations. A practical cut-off between 0.9 to 1.1 was used to flag observations with meaningful impact on precision, although there is no agreed universal alternative cut-off.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/covratio_plot_1.png') 

```

##### Observations of interest identified by the influence plot

```{r, ft.align="left"}

 R_cooks_distance_flextable_1
```

##### Results for outliers and influential points

Three observations had studentized residuals greater than three. Two of these did not make a substantial difference to estimates with their DFBETAS, DFFITS, and COVRATIO statistics were within conventional thresholds. The third observation was an extreme outlier, with a Cook’s distance of approximately 11 and DFBETAS and DFFITS values around three, indicating that both the regression coefficients and their standard errors would change substantially if this observation were removed. The COVRATIO for this observation was approximately 0.6, suggesting that it also affected the precision of the parameter estimates. Residual diagnostics further suggested that this observation may be contributing to violations of the linearity assumption.

##### Checking for normality of the residuals using a Q–Q plot

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/qq_plot_1.png') 
```

##### Normality of residuals using Shapiro-Wilk and Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_normality_ks_flextable_1
```

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_normality_sw_flextable_1
```

##### Normality results

-   `r ifelse(R_normality_ks_raw_df_1$p.value[1] < 0.05, "The Kolmogorov-Smirnov test indicates residuals may not be normally distributed.", "The Kolmogorov-Smirnov supports residuals being normally distributed.")`
-   `r ifelse(R_normality_sw_raw_df_1$p.value[1] < 0.05, "The Shapiro-Wilk normality test indicates residuals may not be normally distributed.", "The Shapiro-Wilk supports residuals being normally distributed.")`
-   `r notnormalQ`

##### Assessing independence with the Durbin–Watson test for autocorrelation

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_independence_flextable_1
```

##### Independence results

-   `r ifelse(R_independence_raw_df_1$p[1] < 0.05, "The Durbin–Watson test suggests there are auto-correlation issues.", "The Durbin–Watson test suggests there are no auto-correlation issues.")`
-   `r indDesNo`

##### Assumption conclusions

The model assumptions were dominated by an extreme outlier, with a Cook’s distance of approximately 11 and DFBETAS and DFFITS values around three, indicating that both the regression coefficients and their standard errors would change substantially if this observation were removed. The COVRATIO for this observation was approximately 0.6, suggesting a material impact on the precision of the parameter estimates. Residual diagnostics further indicated that this observation may be contributing to violations of the linearity assumption.

Residual plots also suggested departures from normality, evidence of autocorrelation, and mild heteroscedasticity. In addition, the study design involved repeated measures, which were not accounted for in the analysis. Taken together, these issues indicate that the fitted model poorly represents the underlying data structure.

### Differences

##### Forest plot showing original and reproduced coefficients and 95% confidence intervals for `r namey1`

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/forest_plot_model_1.png')

```

##### Change in regression coefficients

```{r, ft.align="left"}

CC_model_1
```

##### Change in p-values

```{r, ft.align="left"}
Cp_model_1
```

##### Results for p-values

The p-value for the model was reproduced.

#### Conclusion computational reproducibility

`r CompRep`

### Sensitivity analysis

#### Methods

The model was successfully reproduced; however, residual diagnostics indicated non-linearity and the presence of influential observations that may affect both point estimates and confidence intervals. The data was standardized and fitted with and without the outlying observation to compare the differences in coefficients.

Linear, quadratic, and cubic models were therefore visualised using the same variable transformations as specified in the original model. Model fit was compared using AIC and BIC, with comparisons restricted to models fitted to the same dataset. Models with and without the influential outlier were assessed descriptively through visualisation rather than formal information-criterion comparison. To address assumption violations, the outcome variable (cycle ergometer VO₂) was log-transformed and the model refitted. Robust regression was then applied to reduce the effect of influential observations.

#### Results

After standardisation, the regression coefficients changed only slightly (0.88 with the outlier included vs 0.91 excluded). On this basis alone, one might conclude that the outlier does not substantially affect the results. However, this conclusion would be misleading. The apparent stability of the coefficient arises because the linear model provides a poor fit to the data both with and without the outlier, rather than because the outlier is inconsequential.

```{r}
dat <- dat %>%
  mutate(
    z_Cycle_ergometer_VO2 = scale(Cycle_ergometer_VO2, scale = TRUE, center = TRUE),  
     z_Cycle_ergometer_log_VE = scale(Cycle_ergometer_log_VE, scale = TRUE, center = TRUE),  
    z_Cycle_ergometer_log_VE_sq = (z_Cycle_ergometer_log_VE ^2),  
    z_Cycle_ergometer_log_VE_cube = (z_Cycle_ergometer_log_VE ^3))


datOut <- dat %>% slice(-84) %>% 
  mutate(
    z_Cycle_ergometer_VO2 = scale(Cycle_ergometer_VO2, scale = TRUE, center = TRUE),  
     z_Cycle_ergometer_log_VE = scale(Cycle_ergometer_log_VE, scale = TRUE, center = TRUE),  
    z_Cycle_ergometer_log_VE_sq = (z_Cycle_ergometer_log_VE ^2),  
    z_Cycle_ergometer_log_VE_cube = (z_Cycle_ergometer_log_VE ^3))

zmodel_ols1 <- lm(z_Cycle_ergometer_VO2 ~ z_Cycle_ergometer_log_VE, data = dat)
zmodel_ols_glance1<-glance(zmodel_ols1)
zmy_model_ols1 <- tbl_regression(zmodel_ols1,  intercept = TRUE,  estimate_fun = ~ style_number(.x, digits = 4)) %>% add_n
zmy_modelols1 <- tbl_regression(zmodel_ols1,  intercept = TRUE, estimate_fun = ~ style_number(.x, digits = 4))

zmodel_ols_out1 <- lm(z_Cycle_ergometer_VO2 ~ z_Cycle_ergometer_log_VE, data = datOut)
zmodel_ols_glance1_out<-glance(zmodel_ols_out1)
zmy_model_ols_out1 <- tbl_regression(zmodel_ols_out1,  intercept = TRUE, estimate_fun = ~ style_number(.x, digits = 4)) %>% add_n




model_ols1 <- lm(Cycle_ergometer_VO2 ~ Cycle_ergometer_log_VE, data = dat)
model_ols_glance1<-glance(model_ols1)
my_model_ols1 <- tbl_regression(model_ols1,  intercept = TRUE, estimate_fun = ~ style_number(.x, digits = 4)) %>% add_n
my_modelols1 <- tbl_regression(model_ols1,  intercept = TRUE, estimate_fun = ~ style_number(.x, digits = 4)) 

model_ols1_sq <- lm(Cycle_ergometer_VO2 ~ Cycle_ergometer_log_VE+ I(Cycle_ergometer_log_VE^2), data = dat)
model_ols_glance1_sq<-glance(model_ols1_sq)
model_ols1_cube <- lm(Cycle_ergometer_VO2 ~ Cycle_ergometer_log_VE+ I(Cycle_ergometer_log_VE^2)+ I(Cycle_ergometer_log_VE^3), data = dat)
model_ols_glance1_cube<-glance(model_ols1_cube)


model_ols_out1 <- lm(Cycle_ergometer_VO2 ~ Cycle_ergometer_log_VE, data = datOut)
model_ols_glance1_out<-glance(model_ols_out1)
my_model_ols_out1 <- tbl_regression(model_ols_out1,  intercept = TRUE, estimate_fun = ~ style_number(.x, digits = 4)) %>% add_n

model_ols1_sq_out <- lm(Cycle_ergometer_VO2 ~ Cycle_ergometer_log_VE+ I(Cycle_ergometer_log_VE^2), data = datOut)
model_ols_glance1_sq_out<-glance(model_ols1_sq_out)
model_ols1_cube_out <- lm(Cycle_ergometer_VO2 ~ Cycle_ergometer_log_VE+ I(Cycle_ergometer_log_VE^2)+ I(Cycle_ergometer_log_VE^3), data = datOut)
model_ols_glance1_cube_out<-glance(model_ols1_cube_out)


modfit1 <- bind_rows(model_ols_glance1, model_ols_glance1_sq, model_ols_glance1_cube, model_ols_glance1_out, model_ols_glance1_sq_out, model_ols_glance1_cube_out ) %>% 
mutate(Model_type = c("Linear", "Quadratic", "Cubic","Linear without outlier", "Quadratic without outlier","Cubic without outlier" )) %>%
      relocate(Model_type, .before = 1) %>% mutate(Model=1)

modfit_table<-modfit1 %>%
  mutate( adj.r.squared = round(adj.r.squared, 3)) %>% rename(R2adj=adj.r.squared) %>% 
    select(-r.squared,-p.value, -statistic, -Model)  %>%  flextable %>% autofit


Outlier_comparison <- tbl_merge(
  tbls = list(zmy_model_ols1 , zmy_model_ols_out1),
  tab_spanner = c("All_data", "Outlier Removed")) 
Outlier_comparison
```

```{r}




OM<-ggplot(dat, aes(x = Cycle_ergometer_log_VE, y = Cycle_ergometer_VO2)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  
  # Linear fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x,
    se = TRUE,
    colour = "blue",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  # Quadratic fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x + I(x^2),
    se = TRUE,      # set to FALSE if you want no ribbon
    colour = "red",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  # Cubic fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x + I(x^2) + I(x^3),
    se = TRUE,      # consider FALSE to avoid clutter
    colour = "black",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  labs(
    x = "Log(Cycle Ergometer VE)",
    y = "Cycle Ergometer VO2",
    title = "Linear (blue), quadratic (red), and cubic (black) regression (full data)"
  ) +
  theme_classic()


OMOut<-ggplot(datOut, aes(x = Cycle_ergometer_log_VE, y = Cycle_ergometer_VO2)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +

 # Linear fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x,
    se = TRUE,
    colour = "blue",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  # Quadratic fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x + I(x^2),
    se = TRUE,      # set to FALSE if you want no ribbon
    colour = "red",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  # Cubic fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x + I(x^2) + I(x^3),
    se = TRUE,      # consider FALSE to avoid clutter
    colour = "black",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  labs(
    x = "Log(Cycle Ergometer VE)",
    y = "Cycle Ergometer VO2",
    title = "Linear (blue), quadratic (red), and cubic (black) regression (without oulier)"
  ) +
  theme_classic()





LL<-ggplot(dat, aes(x = Cycle_ergometer_log_VE, y = Cycle_ergometer_log_VO2)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  
  # Linear fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x,
    se = TRUE,
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  
  labs(
    x = "Log(Cycle Ergometer VE)",
    y = "Log(Cycle Ergometer VO2)",
    title = "Linear (blue)" ) +
  theme_classic()


# Robust OLS comparsion

log_model_ols1 <- lm(Cycle_ergometer_log_VO2 ~ Cycle_ergometer_log_VE, data = dat)
fit_lmrob1      <- robustbase::lmrob(Cycle_ergometer_log_VO2 ~ Cycle_ergometer_log_VE, data = dat)

tidy_lm_ci <- function(model) {
  broom::tidy(model, conf.int = TRUE) |>
    dplyr::select(term, estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
    dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(c(estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high), ~ round(.x, 3)))
}

tidy_lmrob_ci_from_model <- function(model) {
  s  <- summary(model)
  ci <- confint(model)  # direct from lmrob

  out <- data.frame(
    term      = rownames(s$coefficients),
    estimate  = s$coefficients[, 1],
    std.error = s$coefficients[, 2],
    p.value   = s$coefficients[, 4],
    conf.low  = ci[, 1],
    conf.high = ci[, 2],
    row.names = NULL
  )

  out |>
    dplyr::select(term, estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
    dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(c(estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high), ~ round(.x, 3)))
}

tab_side_by_side <-
  tidy_lm_ci(log_model_ols1) |>
  dplyr::mutate(p.value = round_p(p.value)) |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    tidy_lmrob_ci_from_model(fit_lmrob1) |>
      dplyr::mutate(p.value = round_p(p.value)) |>
      dplyr::rename_with(~ paste0(.x, "_ROB"), -term),
    by = "term"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    term = dplyr::case_when(
      term == "(Intercept)" ~ "Intercept",
      term == "Cycle_ergometer_log_VE" ~ "Cycle_ergometer_log_VE",
      TRUE ~ term
    )
  )

ft1 <-
  flextable::flextable(tab_side_by_side) |>
  flextable::set_header_labels(
    term = "Variable",
    estimate = "Estimate", std.error = "SE", conf.low = "Lower", conf.high = "Upper", p.value = "p-value",
    estimate_ROB = "Estimate", std.error_ROB = "SE", conf.low_ROB = "Lower", conf.high_ROB = "Upper", p.value_ROB = "p-value"
  ) |>
  flextable::add_header_row(
    values = c("", "Ordinary least squares", "Robust regression"),
    colwidths = c(1, 5, 5)
  ) |>
  flextable::align(j = 1, align = "left", part = "all") |>
  flextable::align(j = 2:ncol(tab_side_by_side), align = "left", part = "all") |>
  flextable::autofit()







```

Going back to the unstandardized data, the initial model had substantial violations of regression assumptions, driven by an extreme influential outlier, as shown by fitting three models: Linear (BIC = -61), quadratic (BIC = -87), and cubic (BIC = -215). The Cubic and quadratic models showed BIC reductions of more than 10; however, none of the models provided an adequate fit. The cubic model appeared to follow individual extreme observations rather than the underlying trend, while both the linear and quadratic fits were artificially pulled downward at the upper end of the predictor range. After removal of the outlier, the linear model remained a poor representation of the data (BIC = -193), with quadratic fitting best (BIC = -230) than cubic (BIC = -224), with residual curvature and continued influence from observations at the upper end of the distribution.

##### Comparison of non linear models with full data

```{r}
OM

```

##### Comparison of non linear models with outlier removed

```{r}
OMOut
```

The full dataset was re-analysed with the outlier retained, applying log-transformations to both the predictor and outcome variables. Visually, the resulting log–log model performed similarly to the quadratic model fitted to the reduced dataset. In the log–log specification, ventilation (VE) was strongly associated with carbon dioxide production (VO₂), with a 1% increase in VE associated with an approximately 2.9% increase in VO₂.

Although the log–log transformation did not substantially improve the residual diagnostics, it visually improved the alignment of the fitted line with the observed data. However, one high-leverage observation exerted disproportionate influence on the OLS slope. Robust regression was therefore examined as a sensitivity analysis, downweighting this observation and producing a fitted line that more closely reflected the central trend of the data. The exponent for VE indicates that VO₂ is proportional to VE raised to the power of 3.4. This implies an elastic relationship, where proportional changes in VE are associated with amplified proportional changes in VO₂. For example, doubling VE would be associated with an approximate 2$^{3.4}$ ≈ 10.6-fold increase in VO₂.

##### Log-log model with comparison of OLS and robust regression

```{r}
ggplot(dat, aes(Cycle_ergometer_log_VE, Cycle_ergometer_log_VO2)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_abline(
    intercept = coef(log_model_ols1)[1],
    slope     = coef(log_model_ols1)[2],
     colour = "blue",
    linetype  = "solid"
  ) +
  geom_abline(
    intercept = coef(fit_lmrob1)[1],
    slope     = coef(fit_lmrob1)[2],
     colour = "red",
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Log(Cycle Ergometer VE)",
    y = "Log(Cycle Ergometer VO2)",
    title = "OLS (blue) vs robust regression (red)"
  ) +
  theme_grey()

```

##### Comparison of Robust regression with full data on log scale

```{r}
ft1
```

\newline

Further modelling is constrained by the absence of participant-level identifiers. Without these identifiers, it is not possible to fit random-effects models or formally investigate whether the observed patterns are driven by within-participant clustering rather than the underlying association.
:::

# Model 2

::: panel-tabset
### Original results

##### Model results for `r namey2`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
O_coefficients_flextable_2

```

##### Fit statistics for `r namey2`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
O_fit_stats_flextable_2

```

##### ANOVA table for `r namey2`

```{r,ft.align="left"}
O_anova_flextable_2
```

### Reproduced results

##### Model results `r namey2`

```{r , ft.align="left"}

R_coefficients_flextable_2
```

##### Model fit for `r namey2`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_fit_stats_flextable_2
```

##### ANOVA table for `r namey2`

```{r, ft.align="left"}

R_anova_flextable_2
```

##### Visualisation of regression model

The blue line shows the best line of fit with shading representing 95% confidence intervals, while holding all other covariates constant. The dots show partial residuals, which reflect the observed data adjusted for all other predictors except the one being plotted.

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/reg_plot_2.png') 

```

##### Checking residuals plots for patterns

Blue line showing quadratic fit for residuals

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/res_plot_2.png') 

```

##### Testing residuals for non linear relationships

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_residuals_flextable_2

```

##### Checking univariate relationships with the dependent variable using scatterplots

Blue line shows linear relationship, red line indicates relationship inferred by GAM modelling

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/scatter_plot_2.png') 

```

##### Linearity results

`r linno`

##### Testing for homoscedasticity

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_homoscedasticity_flextable_2
```

##### Homoscedasticity results

-   `r ifelse(R_homoscedasticity_raw_df_2[1] < 0.05, "The studentized Breusch-Pagan test indicates heteroscedasticity.", "The studentized Breusch-Pagan test supports homoscedasticity.")`
-   `r homYes`

##### Model descriptives including cook's distance and leverage to understand outliers

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_description_flextable_2

```

##### Cooks threshold

Cook's distance measures the overall change in fit, if the *ith* observation is removed. Potential influential observations are identified by $\text{Cook's Distance}_i > \frac{4}{n}$, where *n* is the number of observations. In practice a threshold of 0.5 to 1 is often used to identify influential observations.

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}

knitr::include_graphics('figures/cooks_threshold_2.png')
```

##### DFFIT threshold

DFFIT measures how many standard deviations the fitted values will change when the *ith* observation is removed. Potential influential observations $\left| \text{DFFITS}_i \right| > \frac{2\sqrt{p}}{\sqrt{n}}$ where *p* is the number of predictors (including the intercept), and *n* is the number of observations. In practice, this can result in a large number of points identified, a practical cut-off of 1 was used to flag observations with meaningful impact.

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/dffit_threshold_2.png')

```

##### DFBETA threshold

DFBETAS quantify the influence of the *ith* observation on the *jth* regression coefficient as the change in that coefficient when the observation is omitted, expressed in units of the coefficient’s estimated standard error. There is a DFBETA for each model parameter. Potential influential observations $|\text{DFBETA}_{ij}| > \frac{2}{\sqrt{n}}$, where *n* is the number of observations. In larger datasets, this threshold can flag a high number of observations with only minor influence on the model. A practical cut-off of 1 was used to flag observations with meaningful impact.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/dbetas_threshold_2.png') 

```

##### Influence plot

Observations with high leverage (horizontal) and large residuals (vertical, typically at ±2 or ±3 studentized residuals) are concerning, as they may disproportionately influence the model. This combination is reflected by large bubbles with high Cook's distance indicated by darker shadings of blue.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/cooks_plots_2.png') 

```

##### COVRATIO plot

COVRATIO measures the overall change in the precision (covariance matrix) of the estimated regression coefficients when the ith observation is removed. Values close to 1 indicate little influence on the model’s precision. Values below 1 suggest that an observation inflates the variances and reduces precision, resulting in wider confidence intervals, whereas values above 1 suggest deflated variances and narrower confidence intervals. A commonly cited guideline is $\left|\mathrm{COVRATIO}_i - 1\right| > \frac{3p}{n}$, where *p* is the number of parameters and *n* is the number of observations. A practical cut-off between 0.9 to 1.1 was used to flag observations with meaningful impact on precision, although there is no agreed universal alternative cut-off.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/covratio_plot_2.png') 

```

##### Observations of interest identified by the influence plot

```{r, ft.align="left"}
 R_cooks_distance_flextable_2
```

##### Results for outliers and influential points

-   Although two observations had residuals greater than three Cook’s distances, DFBETAS, DFFITS and COVRATIO were within acceptable limits, suggesting minimal influence on model estimates.

##### Checking for normality of the residuals using a Q–Q plot

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/qq_plot_2.png') 
```

##### Normality of residuals using Shapiro-Wilk and Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_normality_ks_flextable_2
R_normality_sw_flextable_2
```

##### Normality results

-   `r ifelse(R_normality_ks_raw_df_2$p.value[1] < 0.05, "The Kolmogorov-Smirnov test indicates residuals may not be normally distributed.", "The Kolmogorov-Smirnov supports residuals being normally distributed.")`
-   `r ifelse(R_normality_sw_raw_df_2$p.value[1] < 0.05, "The Shapiro-Wilk normality test indicates residuals may not be normally distributed.", "The Shapiro-Wilk supports residuals being normally distributed.")`
-   `r notnormalQ`

##### Assessing independence with the Durbin–Watson test for autocorrelation

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_independence_flextable_2
```

##### Independence results

-   `r ifelse(R_independence_raw_df_2$p[1] < 0.05, "The Durbin–Watson test suggests there are auto-correlation issues.", "The Durbin–Watson test suggests there are no auto-correlation issues.")`
-   `r indDesNo`

##### Assumption conclusions

The residual diagnostics indicated departures from linearity and normality; however, the identified outliers were not considered influential. There was no indication of substantial heteroscedasticity.

### Differences

##### Forest plot showing Original and Reproduced coefficients and 95% confidence intervals for `r namey2`

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/forest_plot_model_2.png')

```

##### Change in regression coefficients

```{r, ft.align="left"}
CC_model_2
```

##### Change in p-values

```{r, ft.align="left"}
Cp_model_2
```

##### Results for p-values

-   The p-value for this model was reproduced.

#### Conclusion computational reproducibility

`r CompRep`

### Sensitivity analysis

#### Methods

The model was successfully reproduced; however, residual diagnostics indicated that the residuals were not normally distributed and non-linear. Linear, quadratic, and cubic models were fit and compared using AIC and BIC and standardize for reporting of coefficients. To address assumption violations, the outcome variable (Half squat VO₂) was log-transformed, and the model was refitted, and the residuals were re-examined.

#### Results

Visual inspection of the linear, quadratic and cubic models suggested pronounced curvature at the lower end of the predictor range, with the linear model being pulled down at the upper end. Model fit statistics indicated substantial improvement for the quadratic model, with reductions in AIC and BIC exceeding 10 relative to the linear model, providing strong evidence that a linear specification was inadequate.

```{r}

dat <- dat %>%
  mutate(
    z_Half_squat_VO2 = scale(Half_squat_VO2, scale = TRUE, center = TRUE),  
     z_Half_squat_log_VE = scale(Half_squat_log_VE, scale = TRUE, center = TRUE),  
    z_Half_squat_log_VE_sq = (z_Half_squat_log_VE ^2),  
    z_Half_squat_log_VE_cube = (z_Half_squat_log_VE^3))



model_ols2 <- lm(z_Half_squat_VO2~ z_Half_squat_log_VE, data = dat)
model_ols_glance2<-glance(model_ols2)
my_model_ols2<-tbl_regression(model_ols2, intercept = TRUE, estimate_fun = ~ style_number(.x, digits = 4))

model_ols2_sq <- lm(z_Half_squat_VO2 ~ z_Half_squat_log_VE+ z_Half_squat_log_VE_sq, data = dat)
model_ols_glance2_sq<-glance(model_ols2_sq)
my_model_ols2_sq<-tbl_regression(model_ols2_sq, intercept = TRUE, estimate_fun = ~ style_number(.x, digits = 4))
model_ols2_cube <- lm(z_Half_squat_VO2 ~ z_Half_squat_log_VE+ z_Half_squat_log_VE_sq+ z_Half_squat_log_VE_cube, data = dat)
model_ols_glance2_cube<-glance(model_ols2_cube)
my_model_ols2_cube<-tbl_regression(model_ols2_cube, intercept = TRUE, estimate_fun = ~ style_number(.x, digits = 4))


modfit2 <- bind_rows(model_ols_glance2, model_ols_glance2_sq, model_ols_glance2_cube ) %>% 
mutate(Model_type = c("Linear", "Quadratic", "Cubic" )) %>%
      relocate(Model_type, .before = 1) %>% mutate(Model=2, paper_ID=paper_ID)

modfit_table2<-modfit2 %>%
  mutate( adj.r.squared = round(adj.r.squared, 3)) %>% rename(R2adj=adj.r.squared) %>% 
    select(-r.squared,-p.value, -statistic, -Model, -paper_ID)  %>%  flextable %>% autofit


non_linear2 <- tbl_merge(
  tbls = list(my_model_ols2, my_model_ols2_sq,my_model_ols2_cube),
  tab_spanner = c("Linear", "Quadratic", "Cubic")) 


OMH<-ggplot(dat, aes(x = Half_squat_log_VE, y = Half_squat_VO2)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  
  # Linear fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x,
    se = TRUE,
    colour = "blue",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  # Quadratic fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x + I(x^2),
    se = TRUE,      # set to FALSE if you want no ribbon
    colour = "red",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  # Cubic fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x + I(x^2) + I(x^3),
    se = TRUE,      # consider FALSE to avoid clutter
    colour = "black",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  labs(
    x = "Log(Half Squat Ergometer VE)",
    y = "Half squat Ergometer VO2",
    title = "Linear (blue),  quadratic (red) and cubic (black) regression"
  ) +
  theme_classic()


NOMH<-ggplot(dat, aes(x = Half_squat_VE, y = Half_squat_VO2)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  
  # Linear fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x,
    se = TRUE,
    colour = "blue",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  # Quadratic fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x + I(x^2),
    se = TRUE,      # set to FALSE if you want no ribbon
    colour = "red",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  # Cubic fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x + I(x^2) + I(x^3),
    se = TRUE,      # consider FALSE to avoid clutter
    colour = "black",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  
  labs(
    x = "Log(Half Squat Ergometer VE)",
    y = "Half squat Ergometer VO2",
    title = "Linear (blue),  quadratic (red) and cubic (black) regression"
  ) +
  theme_classic()




LLH<-ggplot(dat, aes(x = Half_squat_log_VE, y = Half_squat_log_VO2)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  
  # Linear fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x,
    se = TRUE,
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
       labs(
    x = "Log(Half Squat Ergometer VE)",
    y = "Log(Half Squat Ergometer VO2)",
    title = "Linear (blue)" ) +
  theme_classic()

log_model_ols2 <- lm(Half_squat_log_VO2 ~ Half_squat_log_VE,   data = dat)
log_model_ols2_glance<-glance(log_model_ols2)

my_log_model_ols2<-tbl_regression(log_model_ols2, intercept = TRUE, estimate_fun = ~ style_number(.x, digits = 4))



```

##### Comparison of non-linear terms standardized coefficients

```{r}
non_linear2 

```

##### Comparison of non-linear terms orginal scales

```{r, ft.align="left"}
OMH
```

##### Comparison of non-linear terms fit

```{r}
modfit_table2

```

Because the X variable (Half_squat_log_VE) had already been log transformed rather than fitting a further quadratic a more simple solution was to reduce the curvature by also logging the the y variable (Half_squat_VO2). In the log–log model, the exponent for VE indicates that VO₂ is proportional to VE raised to the power of 3.4. This implies an elastic relationship, where proportional changes in VE are associated with amplified proportional changes in VO₂. For example, doubling VE would be associated with an approximate 2$^{2.8}$ ≈ 7-fold increase in VO₂.

##### Model with log coefficients

```{r}
my_log_model_ols2

```

##### Model with log transformed variables

```{r, ft.align="left"}
LLH
```

Residual diagnostics for the log–log model indicated no evidence of residual nonlinearity. Although a small number of observations had relatively large residuals, Cook’s distance values were well below conventional thresholds, suggesting no undue influence on the fitted model. Further modelling is constrained by the absence of participant-level identifiers. Without these identifiers, it is not possible to fit random-effects models or formally investigate whether the observed patterns are driven by within-participant clustering rather than the underlying association.

```{r, results='hide'}
my_res_file <- "figures/residualPlots_model_log_ols2.png"

png(my_res_file, width = 1200, height = 800, res = 150)
car::residualPlots(log_model_ols2, type = "rstandard")
dev.off()
  
```

**Residual plots**

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/residualPlots_model_log_ols2.png')
```

```{r}
cooks_plot <- influencePlot(log_model_ols2, xlab = "Leverage", ylab = "Studentized Residuals")
```
:::

# Model 3

::: panel-tabset
### Original results

##### Model results for `r namey3`

```{r,  ft.align="left"}

O_coefficients_flextable_3

```

##### Fit Statistics `r namey3`

```{r,  ft.align="left"}
O_fit_stats_flextable_3
```

##### ANOVA Table for `r namey3`

```{r,  ft.align="left"}
O_anova_flextable_3

```

### Reproduced results

##### Model results for `r namey3`

```{r,  ft.align="left"}
R_coefficients_flextable_3
```

##### Fit statistics for `r namey3`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_fit_stats_flextable_3
```

##### ANOVA Table for `r namey3`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_anova_flextable_3
```

##### Visualisation of regression model

The blue line shows the best line of fit with shading representing 95% confidence intervals, while holding all other covariates constant. The dots show partial residuals, which reflect the observed data adjusted for all other predictors except the one being plotted.

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/reg_plot_3.png') 

```

##### Checking residuals plots for patterns

Blue line showing quadratic fit for residuals

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/res_plot_3.png') 

```

##### Testing residuals for non linear relationships

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_residuals_flextable_3
```

##### Checking univariate relationships with the dependent variable using scatterplots

Blue line shows linear relationship, red line indicates relationship inferred by GAM modelling

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/scatter_plot_3.png') 

```

##### Linearity results

`r linno`

##### Testing for homoscedasticity

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_homoscedasticity_flextable_3
```

##### Homoscedasticity results

-   `r ifelse(R_homoscedasticity_raw_df_3$p.value[1] < 0.05, "The studentized Breusch-Pagan test indicates heteroscedasticity.", "The studentized Breusch-Pagan test supports homoscedasticity.")`
-   `r homYes`

##### Model descriptives including cook's distance and leverage to understand outliers

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_description_flextable_3

```

##### Cooks threshold

Cook's distance measures the overall change in fit, if the *ith* observation is removed. Potiential influential observations are identified by $\text{Cook's Distance}_i > \frac{4}{n}$, where *n* is the number of observations. In practice a theshold of 0.5 to 1 is often used to identify influential observations.

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}

knitr::include_graphics('figures/cooks_threshold_3.png')
```

##### DFFIT threshold

DFFIT measures how many standard deviations the fitted values will change when the *ith* observation is removed. Potiential influential observations $\left| \text{DFFITS}_i \right| > \frac{2\sqrt{p}}{\sqrt{n}}$ where *p* is the number of predictors (including the intercept) and *n* is the number of observations. In practice this can result in a large number of points identified, a practical cut-off of 1 was used to flag observations with meaningful impact.

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/dffit_threshold_3.png')

```

##### DFBETA threshold

DFBETAS quantify the influence of the *ith* observation on the *jth* regression coefficient as the change in that coefficient when the observation is omitted, expressed in units of the coefficient’s estimated standard error. There is a DFBETA for each parameter in the model. Potiential influential observations $|\text{DFBETA}_{ij}| > \frac{2}{\sqrt{n}}$, where *n* is the number of observations. In larger datasets this threshold can flag a high number of observations with only minor influence on the model. A practical cut-off of 1 was used to flag observations with meaningful impact.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/dbetas_threshold_3.png') 

```

##### Influence plot

Observations with high leverage (horizontal) and large residuals (vertical, typically at ±2 or ±3 studentised residuals) are concerning, as they may disproportionately influence the model. This combination is reflected by large bubbles with high Cook's distance indicated by darker shadings of blue.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/cooks_plots_3.png') 

```

##### COVRATIO plot

COVRATIO measures the overall change in the precision (covariance matrix) of the estimated regression coefficients when the ith observation is removed. Values close to 1 indicate little influence on the model’s precision. Values below 1 suggest that an observation inflates the variances and reduces precision, resulting in wider confidence intervals, whereas values above 1 suggest deflated variances and narrower confidence intervals. A commonly cited guideline is $\left|\mathrm{COVRATIO}_i - 1\right| > \frac{3p}{n}$, where *p* is the number of parameters and *n* is the number of observations. A practical cut-off between 0.9 to 1.1 was used to flag observations with meaningful impact on precision, although there is no agreed universal alternative cutoff.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/covratio_plot_3.png') 

```

```{r, ft.align="left"}

##### Observations of interest identified by the influence plot

 R_cooks_distance_flextable_3
```

##### Results for outliers and influential points

-   Four observations had studentized residuals \> 3. They Cook’s distance, with DFBETAS and DFFITS within conventional ranges. However, two COVRATIO values were substantially \< 1, but potential inflation of standard errors (wider confidence intervals). Therefore, these should be further invesigated.

##### Checking for normality of the residuals using a Q–Q plot

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/qq_plot_3.png') 
```

##### Normality of residuals using Shapiro-Wilk and Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_normality_ks_flextable_3
R_normality_sw_flextable_3
```

##### Normality results

-   `r ifelse(R_normality_ks_raw_df_3$p.value[1] < 0.05, "The Kolmogorov-Smirnov test indicates residuals may not be normally distributed.", "The Kolmogorov-Smirnov supports residuals being normally distributed.")`
-   `r ifelse(R_normality_sw_raw_df_3$p.value[1] < 0.05, "The Shapiro-Wilk normality test indicates residuals may not be normally distributed.", "The Shapiro-Wilk supports residuals being normally distributed.")`
-   `r notnormalQ`

##### Assessing independence with the Durbin–Watson test for autocorrelation

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_independence_flextable_3
```

##### Independence results

-   `r ifelse(R_independence_raw_df_3$p[1] < 0.05, "The Durbin–Watson test suggests there are auto-correlation issues.", "The Durbin–Watson test suggests there are no auto-correlation issues.")`
-   `r indDesNo`

##### Assumption conclusions

Residual plots indicated departures from normality. Diagnostic plots and tests also suggested potential non-linearity, indicating that the fitted linear models may not have fully captured the underlying relationships. COVRATIO values suggested reduced precision of the confidence intervals, and residuals showed evidence of autocorrelation. Given the repeated-measures study design, the assumption of independence was unlikely to hold.

### Differences

##### Forest plot showing original and reproduced coefficients and 95% confidence intervals for `r namey3`

```{r}

knitr::include_graphics('figures/forest_plot_model_3.png')

```

##### Change in regression coefficients

```{r, ft.align="left"}
CC_model_3
```

##### Change in p-values

```{r, ft.align="left"}

Cp_model_3
```

##### Results for p-values

-   The p-value for this model was reproduced.

#### Conclusion computational reproducibility

-   `r CompRep`

### Sensitivity analysis

#### Methods

The model was successfully reproduced; however, residual diagnostics indicated departures from normality and evidence of nonlinearity. The predictors were standardized, and linear, quadratic, and cubic specifications were fitted and compared using AIC and BIC. Model visualisations were presented on the original scale, as specified by the authors. To address assumption violations, variables were log-transformed and the model refitted. Residual curvature persisted following transformation; therefore, quadratic and a spline models were subsequently compared using AIC and BIC to assess relative model fit. A natural cubic spline with three degrees of freedom was used to capture residual curvature after log-transformation, while limiting model complexity and improving stability at the extremes of the predictor range.

#### Results

Visual inspection of the linear, quadratic and cubic models suggested pronounced curvature at the upper end of the predictor range. Model fit statistics indicated substantial improvement for the quadratic model, with reductions in AIC and BIC exceeding 10 relative to the linear model, providing strong evidence that a linear specification was inadequate. Therefore, this model was not inferentially reproducible.

```{r}


dat <- dat %>%
  mutate(
    z_Cycle_Ergometer_VE  = scale(Cycle_Ergometer_VE , scale = TRUE, center = TRUE),  
     z_VCO2 = scale(VCO2, scale = TRUE, center = TRUE),  
    z_VCO2_sq = (z_VCO2^2),  
    z_VCO2_cube = (z_VCO2^3))



model_ols3 <- lm(z_Cycle_Ergometer_VE~ z_VCO2, data = dat)
model_ols_glance3<-glance(model_ols3)
my_model_ols3<-tbl_regression(model_ols3, intercept = TRUE, estimate_fun = ~ style_number(.x, digits = 4))

model_ols3_sq <- lm(z_Cycle_Ergometer_VE ~ z_VCO2+ z_VCO2_sq, data = dat)
model_ols_glance3_sq<-glance(model_ols3_sq)
my_model_ols3_sq<-tbl_regression(model_ols3_sq, intercept = TRUE, estimate_fun = ~ style_number(.x, digits = 4))
model_ols3_cube <- lm(z_Cycle_Ergometer_VE ~ z_VCO2+ z_VCO2_sq+ z_VCO2_cube, data = dat)
model_ols_glance3_cube<-glance(model_ols3_cube)
my_model_ols3_cube<-tbl_regression(model_ols3_cube, intercept = TRUE, estimate_fun = ~ style_number(.x, digits = 4))

non_linear3 <- tbl_merge(
  tbls = list(my_model_ols3, my_model_ols3_sq,my_model_ols3_cube),
  tab_spanner = c("Linear", "Quadratic", "Cubic")) 



modfit3a <- bind_rows(model_ols_glance3, model_ols_glance3_sq, model_ols_glance3_cube ) %>% 
mutate(Model_type = c("Linear", "Quadratic", "Cubic" )) %>%
      relocate(Model_type, .before = 1) %>% mutate(Model=3, paper_ID=paper_ID)

modfit_table3a<-modfit3a %>%
  mutate( adj.r.squared = round(adj.r.squared, 3)) %>% rename(R2adj=adj.r.squared) %>% 
    select(-r.squared,-p.value, -statistic, -Model, -paper_ID)  %>%  flextable %>% autofit


OMC<-ggplot(dat, aes( x = VCO2, y = Cycle_Ergometer_VE)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  
  # Linear fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x,
    se = TRUE,
    colour = "blue",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  # Quadratic fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x + I(x^2),
    se = TRUE,      # set to FALSE if you want no ribbon
    colour = "red",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  # Cubic fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x + I(x^2) + I(x^3),
    se = TRUE,      # consider FALSE to avoid clutter
    colour = "black",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  labs(
    x = "VCO2",
    y = "Cycle Ergometer VE",
    title = "Linear (blue), quadratic (red), and cubic (black) regression"
  ) +
  theme_classic()




LLC<-ggplot(dat, aes(x = log_VCO2, y = Cycle_Ergometer_log_VE)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  
  # Linear fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x,
    se = TRUE,
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  # Quadratic fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ x + I(x^2),
    se = TRUE,      # set to FALSE if you want no ribbon
    colour = "red",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  # spline fit
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ splines::ns(x, df = 3),
    se = TRUE,      # consider FALSE to avoid clutter
    colour = "darkgreen",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  
  
  labs(
    x = "Log(VCO2)",
    y = "Log(Cycle_Ergometer VE)",
    title = "Linear (blue) and quadratic (red), Spline (green)" ) +
  theme_classic()

log_model_ols3 <- lm(Cycle_Ergometer_log_VE ~  log_VCO2 ,   data = dat)
log_model_ols3_glance<-glance(log_model_ols3)
log_model_ols3_sq <- lm(Cycle_Ergometer_log_VE ~ log_VCO2 + I(log_VCO2 ^2),   data = dat)
log_model_ols3_sq_glance<-glance(log_model_ols3_sq)
log_model_ols3_cube <- lm(Cycle_Ergometer_log_VE ~ log_VCO2 + I(log_VCO2 ^2) + I(log_VCO2 ^3),   data = dat)
log_model_ols3_cube_glance<-glance(log_model_ols3_cube)


mod_ns3 <- lm(log(Cycle_Ergometer_VE) ~ ns(log(VCO2), df = 3),   data = dat)
mod_ns3_glance<-glance(mod_ns3)
spline_result<-tidy(summary(mod_ns3)) %>% mutate(p.value=round_p(p.value)) %>% flextable %>% autofit %>% colformat_double(digits = 3)



modfit3b <- bind_rows( log_model_ols3_glance, log_model_ols3_sq_glance, mod_ns3_glance ) %>% 
mutate(Model_type = c("log Linear", "log Quadratic", "Log Spline" )) %>%
      relocate(Model_type, .before = 1) %>% mutate(Model=3, paper_ID=paper_ID)

modfit_table3b<-modfit3b %>%
  mutate( adj.r.squared = round(adj.r.squared, 3)) %>% rename(R2adj=adj.r.squared) %>% 
    select(-r.squared,-p.value, -statistic, -Model, -paper_ID)  %>%  flextable %>% autofit

modfit3 <- bind_rows(modfit3a, modfit3b)





```

##### Comparison of non-linear terms standardized coefficients

```{r}
non_linear3 

```

##### Comparison of non-linear terms orginal scales

```{r, ft.align="left"}
OMC
```

##### Fit comparison of non-linear

```{r}
modfit_table3a
```

To reduce curvature, both the predictor and outcome variables were log-transformed. Residual diagnostics indicated some remaining non-linearity. Quadratic and spline models were therefore fitted and compared with the linear model using AIC and BIC, both of which showed substantially improved fit (ΔAIC and ΔBIC \> 10). The spline model provided a marginally better fit than the quadratic model (ΔAIC = 6.6; ΔBIC = 2.6) and was selected as the final model, after visual inspection.

##### Comparison log-log model

```{r, ft.align="left"}
LLC
```

```{r}
modfit_table3b
```

The spline terms were all statistically significant; however, individual spline coefficients are not directly interpretable. The fitted spline indicated a clear non-linear association between log(VCO₂) and log(ventilation).

Further modelling is constrained by the absence of participant-level identifiers. Without these identifiers, it is not possible to fit random-effects models or formally investigate whether the observed patterns are driven by within-participant clustering rather than the underlying association. \newline

##### Spline fit

```{r}

ggplot(dat, aes(x = log(VCO2), y = log(Cycle_Ergometer_VE))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(
    method = "lm",
    formula = y ~ splines::ns(x, df = 3),
    se = TRUE,
     colour = "darkgreen",
  ) +
  theme_classic()
```

##### Spline results

```{r}
spline_result
```
:::

```{r}
Assumptions_data<- bind_rows(M1Rassump, M2Rassump,M3Rassump)
modfit<- bind_rows(modfit1, modfit2, modfit3)
write.csv(modfit, file="data/modfit_46.csv")
write.csv(Assumptions_data ,file="data/Assumptions_data46.csv")
write.csv(fdat,file="data/model_data46.csv")
```
